conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me first review the official eligibility rules so I can guide you properly.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for your patience. I've reviewed the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit. 

To assess your eligibility, I need to ask you some questions. Let me start with the first one:

**Do you have a child or children that you're responsible for?** If yes, could you please tell me their names and ages?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I'd like to find out if I can claim Child Benefit. I have one child - my son Alex, he's 16 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I understand you have a son named Alex who is 16 years old.

Since Alex is 16, his eligibility for Child Benefit depends on whether he's staying in approved education or training. 

**Is Alex currently in approved education or training** (such as school, college, or an approved training course)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not in any education or training at the moment. He finished school but hasn't started anything else yet.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for letting me know. That's helpful information.

**Has Alex registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services?** If yes, when did he register and when did he leave education?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he hasn't registered with the careers service or the armed services. He just finished school and hasn't signed up for anything like that.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. I have a few more questions to complete the assessment.

**Is Alex currently working?** If so, how many hours per week does he work?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't working at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. Just a couple more questions to complete the assessment:

**Is Alex receiving any benefits in his own name**, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he doesn't get any benefits in his own name.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Just a few more questions:

**Does Alex live with you in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives at home with me, and yes, I live in the UK - we're in Manchester.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. And finally:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. I now have enough details to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Let me carefully review the rules based on what you've told me:

**Assessment for Alex:**

According to the official guidance, you normally qualify for Child Benefit if you're responsible for a child under 16 and you live in the UK. For children aged 16 or over, you'll normally still qualify if your child is under 20 **and** they stay in approved education or training.

Additionally, you can apply for Child Benefit to continue for **20 weeks** if a 16 or 17 year old leaves education or training and registers with either a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services.

In your situation:
- Alex is 16 years old
- He is not in approved education or training
- He has not registered with a careers service or the armed services
- He is not working 24+ hours per week
- He is not receiving benefits in his own name

Since Alex has left education, is not in approved training, and has not registered with a careers service or the armed services, he does not meet the criteria for Child Benefit to continue beyond age 16.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**I'm sorry, but based on the information you've provided, you are not currently eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.**

Here's why: Child Benefit normally stops when a child turns 16, unless they continue in approved education or training (which can extend it until they're 20). Since Alex has left school and is not in any approved education or training, the standard eligibility criteria are not met.

**However, there may be options available:**

If Alex were to register with a government-sponsored careers service, you could potentially claim Child Benefit for up to 20 weeks after he left education. This would give him some time to find work or decide on further training while you continue to receive support.

If Alex decides to return to education, start an approved training course, or register with a careers service in the future, you may be able to claim Child Benefit again at that point.

I hope this information is helpful. If Alex's circumstances change, you may want to reassess your eligibility at that time.

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 16 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 16 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 16 and not in approved education or extension period
circumstances: Child is 16 and not in approved education or extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 16 years old and has left school. According to the eligibility rules, Child Benefit normally ends when a child turns 16 unless they stay in approved education or training (which can extend eligibility until age 20). Additionally, Child Benefit can continue for 20 weeks if a 16 or 17 year old leaves education or training and registers with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services. Alex is not currently in approved education or training, and has not registered with a careers service or the armed services. Therefore, he does not meet the criteria for Child Benefit eligibility for children aged 16 and over.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex, who is 16 years old. Alex has left education and is not in approved training, not registered with careers services or armed services, not working, and not claiming benefits. Since he does not meet any of the criteria that allow Child Benefit to continue beyond age 16, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
